preFix.title: Red wine gravy
preFix.subtitle: Built on roast pan drippings
preFix.excerpt: A proper roast gravy made from the sticky brown residue left in the roasting pan, deglazed with red wine and enriched with beef or chicken stock. Ready in 10 minutes while the joint rests.
preFix.sourceNotes:
Mrs Beeton, Book of Household Management (1861), Project Gutenberg #10136. Brown gravies and pan sauces.

Eliza Acton, Modern Cookery for Private Families (1845), Project Gutenberg. Roast gravy methods.

content.text: Red wine gravy is made from the roasting tin, not from a separate pan. The brown residue stuck to the base of the tin after roasting meat is pure flavour. Wine loosens it; stock builds it into a proper gravy.
Make it while the joint rests. The timing is natural: 10 minutes for the gravy, 30 minutes for the rest.

items.name: Beef stock
items.prepNote: hot; or chicken stock for poultry

content.text: If you roasted the joint on a bed of onion, carrot, and celery, leave them in the tin while you make the gravy. They add extra flavour and body, and you strain them out at the end.
Build the gravy in the roasting tin
Once the joint is removed to rest, pour off most of the fat from the tin, leaving behind about 2 tablespoons and all the brown sediment.
Set the tin over two hob burners on medium heat. Stir in the flour and cook for 1 minute, scraping up any sticky bits.
Pour in the red wine and
the tin, scraping up all the brown residue. Let it bubble and reduce by half, about 2 minutes.
Pour in the hot stock and add the thyme if using. Bring to a simmer and cook for 6 to 8 minutes, stirring occasionally, until the gravy has thickened slightly and tastes rounded.
Add the Worcestershire sauce if using. Taste and adjust seasoning. Strain through a fine-mesh sieve into a warm jug.

items.fix: Add a splash of Worcestershire sauce or a small knob of butter stirred in at the end. A good roast should produce a well-coloured residue.
items.cause: The roasting tin had no real residue, or the stock was weak.

items.fix: Stir the flour into the fat first and cook for a full minute before adding any liquid. Add the wine gradually, stirring.
items.cause: The flour formed lumps before the liquid was added.

items.fix: Simmer uncovered for a few more minutes. The gravy should coat a spoon.
items.cause: Not enough reduction, or too much stock added.

items.fix: Reduce the wine by at least half before adding stock. The alcohol should cook out completely.
items.cause: The wine was not reduced enough before the stock went in.

content.text: For a port gravy to serve with game or duck, replace the red wine with port and reduce by two thirds before adding the stock. For a lighter chicken gravy, use white wine and chicken stock in the same method.

The pan-dripping gravy is older than the Sunday roast itself. Victorian cookery writer Mrs Beeton describes it as the natural consequence of good roasting: the dripping is the flavour, and the cook who discards it is discarding the best part of the meal. It survives as standard practice because it works, and because it is the only sauce that has the exact flavour of the meat it accompanies.
